# LeetCode
<p>
  <a href="https://leetcode.com/">
    <img src="_Misc/Images/LeetCode.png" width="850" alt="LeetCode website">
  </a>
</p>

LeetCode is a widely used platform for technical interview preparation and algorithmic problem-solving practice. It's commonly used by students and job seekers to build strong fundamentals in data structures and algorithms, improve coding speed/accuracy, and get comfortable with real interview-style questions. <br>
<br>

This GitHub repo collects our LeetCode materials in one place (solutions, practice sets, references, and step-by-step guides) <br>


# !!! DISCLAIMER !!!
- This collection is not guaranteed to be complete.
- Some problems may contain answer keys or solutions, but accuracy is not guaranteed. Please use them as a reference only.
- Always verify ideas using official LeetCode statements, constraints, and your own testing/analysis.
- We DO NOT CONDONE dishonest use (copy-pasting during interviews, online assessments, or any process where it violates rules/policies). Use this repo responsibly. We are not liable for any consequences arising from misuse.

Hope this helps anyone who's grinding interviews and trying to level up!

<br><br>

# Topic-Based Collections

Problems below are grouped by the official LeetCode topic tags found in each generated editorial. A problem can appear in multiple categories when LeetCode tags it with multiple concepts. <br>

<a id="topic-backtracking"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Backtracking</strong> (72 problems)</summary>

*Backtracking explores choices step by step, undoing a choice when it cannot lead to a valid answer. It is common in permutations, combinations, subsets, grids, and constraint-search problems.*

<a id="topic-biconnected-component"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Biconnected Component</strong> (1 problems)</summary>

*Biconnected components are maximal graph regions that stay connected even after removing a single vertex. These problems usually involve articulation points, DFS low-link values, and graph robustness.*

| Problems | Difficulty | Editorial | Solutions |
|----------|:----------:|:---------:|:---------:|
| [1192. Critical Connections in a Network](https://leetcode.com/problems/critical-connections-in-a-network/) | <img src="_Misc/Badges/Hard.svg" height="20" align="absmiddle"> | [Editorial](Editorial/1101-1200/1192_Critical_Connections_in_a_Network) | - |
</details>

<a id="topic-binary-tree"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Binary Tree</strong> (144 problems)</summary>

*A binary tree is a tree where each node has at most two children. Problems often focus on traversal, recursion, depth, paths, and relationships between parent and child nodes.*

<a id="topic-depth-first-search"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Depth-First Search</strong> (233 problems)</summary>

<a id="topic-heap-priority-queue"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Heap (Priority Queue)</strong> (120 problems)</summary>

<a id="topic-sliding-window"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Sliding Window</strong> (92 problems)</summary>

*Sliding window maintains information about a moving contiguous range. It is useful for subarray or substring problems with constraints that can be updated incrementally.*

<a id="topic-strongly-connected-component"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Strongly Connected Component</strong> (2 problems)</summary>

*Strongly connected components are groups of directed graph nodes where every node can reach every other node. They are useful for condensation graphs, cycles, and dependency reasoning.*

| Problems | Difficulty | Editorial | Solutions |

<a id="topic-union-find"></a>
<details close><summary><strong>Union-Find</strong> (70 problems)</summary>

*Union-Find, or Disjoint Set Union, tracks connected components under merge operations. It is useful for connectivity, cycle detection, Kruskal-style MSTs, and grouping problems.*
